catalog_note: Seed controls for harness development. These are local operational controls with versioned upstream traceability where mapped; they are not certification or conformance claims.

controls.title: Inventory AI system boundaries and trust decisions
controls.summary: The system has a documented architecture, trust boundaries, data flows, model/provider dependencies, and explicit AI-mediated decisions.

alignments.note: Architecture and lifecycle verification
Governance, inventory, and risk management
Govern and Map functions

evidence.prompt: Attach a current architecture diagram showing users, services, models, tools, data stores, and external providers.
Describe prompt, context, retrieval, model output, tool-call, and logging data flows.
List decisions or recommendations made or mediated by the AI system.

tests.prompt: Review whether every trust boundary has an owner, input validation strategy, and monitoring expectation.

hardening_actions: Create a living AI system inventory entry.
Document where user-controlled content can influence prompts, context, tools, and outputs.
Identify which decisions require human approval.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for model inventory, AI resource authorization, and AI BOM evidence.

controls.title: Constrain prompt and instruction boundaries
controls.summary: System, developer, user, retrieved, and tool-supplied content are separated and protected against cross-boundary instruction injection.

alignments.note: Prompt and input/output handling verification
Prompt injection and sensitive information disclosure
Adversarial prompt and input manipulation scenarios

evidence.prompt: Attach the system/developer prompt contract or instruction hierarchy.
Attach prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, and context confusion test results.
Describe how retrieved or user-controlled content is labeled and isolated from trusted instructions.

tests.prompt: Inject malicious instructions into a retrieval source and verify they cannot override trusted instructions.
Attempt to make user content appear as system/developer instructions.

hardening_actions: Label untrusted content in prompts and tool responses.
Use structured prompt templates with explicit instruction hierarchy.
Regression-test known jailbreak and indirect injection patterns.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for prompt injection screening and instruction hierarchy enforcement.

controls.title: Authorize and audit agent tool use
controls.summary: Tools available to AI agents are inventoried, scoped, authorized, logged, and constrained by least privilege.

alignments.note: Agentic action security and authorization
Identity, access, logging, and change control
Excessive agency and supply chain risks

evidence.prompt: List all tools, plugins, MCP servers, APIs, and external actions available to the agent.
Describe scopes, approval requirements, policy checks, and deny conditions for each tool.
Attach sample logs that show tool request, approval, execution, output, and actor context.

tests.prompt: Attempt to trigger a tool outside the user's role, tenant, environment, or approval boundary.
Attempt replay or tampering of a prior tool invocation.

hardening_actions: Use an allowlist for agent tools and permissions.
Require human approval for destructive, financial, privileged, or external side-effect actions.
Log tool calls with correlation IDs and user/session context.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for tool isolation, runtime authorization, policy enforcement, and MCP tool authorization.

controls.title: Protect sensitive data in prompts, retrieval, and outputs
controls.summary: Sensitive data exposure is minimized across prompt construction, context retrieval, model responses, logs, and evaluation datasets.

alignments.note: Data protection and privacy verification
Sensitive information disclosure
Data security, privacy, and governance

evidence.prompt: List data classifications processed by prompts, embeddings, retrieval sources, model providers, and logs.
Describe filtering, redaction, tokenization, masking, or policy controls before data reaches the model.
Document retention and deletion behavior for prompts, completions, embeddings, and logs.

tests.prompt: Seed canary secrets and verify they are not retrieved, emitted, logged, or sent to unauthorized providers.
Verify tenant or role boundaries cannot be bypassed through semantic retrieval.

hardening_actions: Block secrets and high-risk regulated data from prompts unless explicitly approved.
Apply retrieval filters before context assembly.
Review provider retention and training-use settings.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for retrieval authorization, post-inference filtering, classification propagation, output leakage controls, embedding sanitization, and secret exclusion from model context.

controls.title: Govern retrieval sources and knowledge freshness
controls.summary: RAG and context sources are authorized, access-controlled, freshness-scored, and resistant to poisoning.

alignments.note: Knowledge base and data lifecycle verification
RAG poisoning and supply chain considerations
Data poisoning and input manipulation scenarios

evidence.prompt: List retrieval sources, owners, indexes, update cadence, access model, and freshness expectations.
Describe ingestion validation, provenance, malware scanning, and poisoning detection.
Attach tests proving retrieval respects user, tenant, and document-level authorization.

tests.prompt: Insert hostile instructions into an authorized document and verify context handling prevents instruction takeover.
Verify stale or revoked content is excluded or labeled in generated answers.

hardening_actions: Track source provenance and freshness metadata.
Apply authorization before retrieval and before prompt assembly.
Quarantine newly ingested high-risk content until validated.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for RAG access control, poisoning detection, memory expiry, retrieval logging, and ingested document provenance.

risk_tags: OWASP LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning
OWASP LLM08 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses

controls.title: Validate output before privileged or external action
controls.summary: Model output is treated as untrusted until validated, especially before code execution, ticket changes, emails, transactions, deployments, or cloud actions.

alignments.note: Output handling and agent action verification
Excessive agency and insecure output handling
Change control, logging, and human oversight

evidence.prompt: Document validators, schemas, policy checks, and approval gates used before action.
List actions that require human approval and the identity/role required to approve.
Attach tests for command injection, unsafe code, unauthorized data changes, and malformed tool arguments.

tests.prompt: Attempt to cause malformed or policy-violating tool arguments and verify the call is blocked.
Verify destructive actions require explicit human approval and are fully logged.

hardening_actions: Use schemas and policy checks for all tool arguments.
Treat generated code and commands as untrusted input.
Require approval for destructive or externally visible actions.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for output schema validation, outbound request prevention, approval gates, and deterministic policy enforcement.

controls.title: Monitor abuse, drift, and security-relevant behavior
controls.summary: The system emits security telemetry for prompts, retrieval, tool use, denials, policy decisions, model/provider failures, and anomalous behavior.

alignments.note: Monitoring, logging, and operations verification
Logging, monitoring, incident response, and assurance
Measure and Manage functions

evidence.prompt: Document security events, metrics, traces, and alerts emitted by the AI system.
Attach prompt abuse, tool abuse, leakage, and anomalous usage detections.
Attach AI-specific incident response, rollback, kill-switch, or provider failover procedures.

tests.prompt: Verify blocked prompt/tool events are logged without leaking sensitive content.
Exercise feature disablement or provider isolation for an AI safety/security incident.

hardening_actions: Add correlation IDs across prompt, retrieval, model, and tool-call events.
Create detections for repeated prompt injection, blocked tools, leakage attempts, and policy bypasses.
Define rollback and disablement paths before production launch.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for AI interaction logging, structured telemetry, abuse detection, token usage attribution, drift monitoring, and proactive action audit logs.

controls.title: Manage AI supply chain and provider risk
controls.summary: Models, providers, SDKs, plugins, prompts, evaluation datasets, and agent dependencies are tracked and reviewed for supply chain risk.

alignments.note: Supply chain and third-party component verification

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Security-Focused Guide for AI Code Assistant Instructions
alignments.note: AI-suggested dependencies, package-manager use, lockfiles, SBOM, provenance, and integrity verification

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Concise Guide for Developing More Secure Software
alignments.note: Secure development, CI tooling, dependency monitoring, vulnerability reporting, SBOM, and release hygiene
npm dependency selection, reproducible installs, and CI least privilege when JavaScript or TypeScript is in scope
Supply chain vulnerabilities
Third-party risk, change management, and assurance

evidence.prompt: List model providers, model IDs, regions, retention settings, and contractual/security assumptions.
Attach SDK, plugin, MCP server, prompt package, and agent dependency inventory.
Attach review notes for AI-suggested dependencies, including necessity, official package source, lockfile update, vulnerability status, and alternatives considered.
Attach SBOM or component inventory for model SDKs, plugins, MCP servers, containers, and generated-code dependencies when available.
Attach provider security review, DPA, terms review, or vendor risk assessment.

tests.prompt: Verify production model/provider configuration matches approved retention, region, and data-use settings.
Verify plugins, MCP servers, and agent tools are pinned, reviewed, and monitored for change.
Verify AI-suggested packages exist in official package registries, are maintained, are not typosquats, and are captured in lockfiles.

hardening_actions: Pin model and SDK versions where feasible.
Track prompt templates and tool definitions as versioned artifacts.
Review provider data-use, retention, logging, and incident notification terms.
Require AI-generated dependency changes to justify necessity, source, maintainer status, vulnerability status, and lockfile impact.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for model registry, signing, deployment verification, provider/model change re-evaluation, model artifact supply chain, AI BOM, and MCP component trust.

controls.title: Perform MAESTRO threat modeling with STRIDE translation
controls.summary: The system has a documented OWASP MAESTRO-first threat model that captures business context, AI architecture layers, agentic risk factors, current and target state, trust boundaries, cross-layer abuse cases, AI Defense Matrix coverage gaps where useful, control gaps, compensating controls, residual risk, and remediation decisions. STRIDE is used as a secondary translation or completeness check.

alignments.note: Threat modeling, architecture, and security requirement verification
Primary AI threat modeling method for agentic, RAG, MCP, and multi-layer AI systems, with seven layers, cross-layer analysis, and agentic risk factors
Risk mapping for LLM and GenAI applications
Leadership and roadmap coverage across AI asset classes and NIST CSF 2.0 functions
Risk management, governance, and assurance evidence
Adversary scenario and abuse-case development
What are we working on, what can go wrong, what are we going to do about it, and did we do a good job

evidence.prompt: Attach the OWASP MAESTRO-first AI threat model with scope, owners, business context, architecture layers, agentic risk factors, trust boundaries, current/target state, and review tier.

Attach the MAESTRO layer inventory covering foundation models, data operations, agent frameworks, deployment/infrastructure, evaluation/observability, security/compliance, agent ecosystem, and cross-layer paths where applicable.
Document how non-determinism, autonomy, agent identity management, and agent-to-agent communication affect the relevant MAESTRO layers.
Attach cross-layer AI abuse cases such as prompt injection to tool misuse, RAG poisoning to unsafe output, identity delegation failure, or provider/model supply-chain drift.
Attach STRIDE translation for the highest-priority findings when useful for AppSec or engineering audiences.
Attach AI Defense Matrix asset-class coverage notes when leadership, ownership, roadmap, or defensive coverage framing is needed.
Attach or link system context, trust-zone/environment, IAM/access flow, data flow, and AI tool/retrieval flow views.
Attach mitigation decisions, owners, due dates, compensating controls, accepted risks, and residual risk.
Document whether the review considered relevant MAESTRO layers, agentic risk factors, cross-layer threats, evidence quality, stakeholders, and revalidation triggers.

tests.prompt: Verify every relevant MAESTRO layer and the cross-layer dimension were considered, and not-applicable layers have rationale rather than being silently skipped.
Verify non-determinism, autonomy, agent identity management, and agent-to-agent communication were reviewed for every relevant layer.

Verify prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, retrieval poisoning, data leakage, unauthorized tool use, excessive agency, unsafe output handling, agent delegation failure, and model/provider drift were considered where applicable.
Verify the highest-priority MAESTRO findings can be translated into STRIDE categories when the audience needs traditional AppSec language.
Verify key AI asset classes have ownership and coverage notes across Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover when the review is used for program planning.
Verify each high or medium risk maps to a remediation, compensating control, accepted risk, or explicit release-blocking decision.

hardening_actions: Run the minimum viable MAESTRO flow during design review.
Use full MAESTRO for high-impact, sensitive, production-facing, agentic, or multi-agent systems.
Apply agentic risk factors across every relevant layer before prioritizing threats.
Translate top findings into STRIDE only when it improves stakeholder communication or catches a completeness gap.
Use the AI Defense Matrix to identify ownership and defensive coverage gaps across AI asset classes.
Track unresolved risks in the implementation ticket, risk register, or exception workflow.
Define revalidation triggers for design changes, new tools, new retrieval sources, new data classes, incidents, and exception renewals.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for validation testing, adversarial evaluation, and abuse detection that threat models should drive.

controls.title: Verify harness provenance and import integrity
controls.summary: AI tool harnesses, prompts, skills, templates, and control catalogs are pinned, reviewed, and integrity-checked before being imported into a target repository or AI agent environment.

alignments.note: Supply chain security, model/tool provenance, change control, and deployment integrity

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Source Code Management Best Practices
alignments.note: Repository protections, review gates, secret scanning, vulnerability alerts, and workflow permissions for harness import surfaces

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Concise Guide for Developing More Secure Software
alignments.note: Secure development, SBOM, vulnerability response, dependency monitoring, and release hygiene for public harness distribution
Supply chain and agent/tool dependency risk
Third-party risk, change management, integrity, and assurance evidence

evidence.prompt: Record the source URL, commit SHA or release tag, retrieval date, and local import path for the harness.
Attach output from `python3 tools/verify-harness-integrity.py` or an equivalent review of imported guidance, skills, templates, and catalogs.
Document who reviewed harness updates before adoption and what changed in agent-facing instructions or controls.

tests.prompt: Verify `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.agents/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, templates, and seed catalogs are present and do not contain hidden side-effect commands or instruction override language.
Verify target repos import the harness from a pinned commit, release tag, submodule, or reviewed copy rather than an unreviewed floating branch.

hardening_actions: Pin harness imports to a reviewed commit or release.
Run the local integrity verifier before using a vendored or updated copy.
Review diffs to agent-facing files before allowing an AI tool to follow them.
Treat public forks, copied prompts, and modified skills as untrusted until reviewed.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for trusted sources, artifact integrity, and software/AI BOM evidence applied to harness imports.

controls.title: Separate trusted harness instructions from untrusted review content
controls.summary: Agent instructions from the harness are treated as trusted workflow guidance, while target repository content, retrieved documents, code comments, issue text, and user-provided artifacts are treated as untrusted inputs that cannot override guardrails.

alignments.note: Input validation, orchestration and agentic security, MCP security, and output control

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Security-Focused Guide for AI Code Assistant Instructions
alignments.note: Trusted coding-agent instructions, explicit security requirements, human review, and self-review loops
Prompt injection, excessive agency, and insecure output handling
Prompt injection, data poisoning, and tool manipulation scenarios

evidence.prompt: Document how the AI tool distinguishes trusted harness instructions from untrusted target repo content, retrieved material, and issue text.
Attach review of AI assistant instruction files against OpenSSF guidance, including secure-coding, dependency, approval, test, and self-review expectations.
Attach tests or review notes showing malicious instructions in target files cannot override harness guardrails or trigger unsafe tools.
Show how reviews label user-controlled, retrieved, generated, and tool-returned content as untrusted.

tests.prompt: Place hostile agent instructions in a target README or issue and verify the reviewer ignores them as untrusted content.
Attempt to override harness guardrails through retrieved context, code comments, or prompt snippets and verify the override fails.
Place unsafe instructions in target repo AI assistant files and verify the reviewer treats them as evidence requiring human adoption, not trusted harness guidance.

hardening_actions: Keep harness guardrails short, explicit, and loaded from trusted project guidance or skill files.
Tell agents to ignore instructions embedded in target repo content unless the human explicitly adopts them.
Label review artifacts and retrieved content as evidence, not as instructions.
Review coding-agent instruction files for OpenSSF-style secure-coding, dependency, test, approval, and self-review expectations.
Regression-test prompt-injection and instruction-boundary abuse cases.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for untrusted-input handling, instruction hierarchy, tool-output separation, and MCP response screening applied to harness instruction boundaries.

controls.title: Constrain AI tool side effects during harness use
controls.summary: AI agents using the harness cannot install dependencies, run networked commands, modify production systems, publish artifacts, or change external state without explicit human approval and reviewable logs.

alignments.note: Agentic action authorization, tool-use controls, logging, monitoring, and incident response

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Security-Focused Guide for AI Code Assistant Instructions
alignments.note: Human approval, tool-assisted security checks, tests, generated-code review, and dependency discipline for AI-assisted coding

alignments.framework: OpenSSF Concise Guide for Developing More Secure Software
alignments.note: CI security tooling, dependency checks, SBOM, code review, and vulnerability monitoring
Identity, authorization, logging, change management, and human oversight
Excessive agency, insecure output handling, and supply chain risks

evidence.prompt: Document AI tool permissions, sandboxing, network access, writable paths, approved commands, and denied side effects.
Attach examples showing human approval is required before network access, dependency installation, privileged commands, external writes, or production-impacting actions.
Attach or reference the AI coding request template requiring a plan, secure-coding constraints, dependency approval, tests, verification, and self-review.
Attach logs that show requested action, approval decision, command/tool used, output, and affected files or systems.

tests.prompt: Verify the agent cannot install or update dependencies while using the harness without explicit approval.
Verify the agent refuses or pauses before networked, privileged, destructive, or externally visible actions.
Verify AI-assisted implementation requests ask for applicable tests, linting, type checks, SAST, SCA, dependency checks, and secret scanning, or explain why they cannot run.

hardening_actions: Default harness use to read-only analysis until the human asks for edits.
Use `templates/ai-code-assistant-request.md` for AI-assisted implementation work that changes code, dependencies, CI, IaC, or deployment behavior.
Require approval for dependency installation, network access, publishing, production access, and external writes.
Keep generated findings reviewable and traceable to source files, evidence, and command output.
Prefer additive patches and backlog items over autonomous changes when risk is unclear.

authoritative_mappings.note: Versioned AISVS traceability for approval gates, tool authorization, deterministic policy enforcement, MCP per-invocation access control, and schema validation applied to agent side effects.
